tasks.title: Calculate Sum of Dynamically Allocated Integers
tasks.description:
In C programming, fixed-size arrays have a major limitation: their size must be known at compile time. However, many real-world applications require arrays whose sizes are determined during program execution, based on user input or data. Dynamic memory allocation, using functions like `malloc`, allows us to create arrays of arbitrary sizes at runtime, providing flexibility and efficient memory usage.

Your task is to write a C program that calculates the sum of a sequence of integers. First, read an integer `N` from standard input, representing the number of integers that will follow. Then, dynamically allocate an array of `N` integers. Read the `N` integers into this dynamically allocated array. Finally, calculate the sum of all elements in the array and print the sum to standard output. Ensure that all dynamically allocated memory is freed before the program exits. `N` will be a positive integer (1 <= N <= 100).

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: 3\n10\n20\n30 → sum: 10 → 30 → 60. Output: 60

tasks.title: Reverse a Dynamically Allocated String
tasks.description:
Strings in C are character arrays, and pointers offer powerful ways to manipulate them flexibly. When operations like reversing text are needed, using dynamic memory allocation allows us to create new strings of exact required lengths at runtime, avoiding fixed-size buffer limitations and making memory management more efficient.

Your task is to write a C program that reads a single word (string) from standard input. Dynamically allocate memory for a new string of the same length to store its reversed version. Reverse the input string and store the result in this newly allocated memory. Finally, print the reversed string to standard output, followed by a newline. Assume the input string will consist only of lowercase English letters and its length will not exceed 100 characters. All dynamically allocated memory must be freed before the program exits.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: hello → reversed: o → ol → oll → olle → olleh. Output: olleh

tasks.title: Sum Diagonals of a Dynamically Allocated Square Matrix
tasks.description:
In scientific computing and image processing, matrices (2D arrays) are fundamental. Representing them with pointers to pointers allows for dynamic allocation of both rows and columns at runtime. This flexibility is crucial when the dimensions of a matrix are not known until the program executes, enabling efficient memory usage for various matrix sizes.

Your task is to write a C program that calculates the sum of elements on the main and anti-diagonals of a square matrix. First, read an integer `N` (1 <= N <= 10) representing the size of an `N x N` square matrix. Then, dynamically allocate memory for this 2D integer matrix. Read `N*N` integers to fill the matrix row by row. Calculate the sum of elements on the main diagonal (top-left to bottom-right) and the anti-diagonal (top-right to bottom-left). If `N` is odd, the center element is part of both diagonals and should only be counted once in the total sum. Print the total sum to standard output. All dynamically allocated memory must be freed before the program exits.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input N=3; Matrix: 1 2 3, 4 5 6, 7 8 9 → main_diag_sum: 1 → 6 → 15. anti_diag_sum: 3 → 8 → 15. total_sum: 1+5+9 + 3+5+7 - 5 = 15 + 15 - 5 = 25. Output: 25

tasks.title: Collect Numbers into a Resizable Array
tasks.description:
Static arrays have a fixed size, which can be inefficient if the exact number of elements is unknown at compile time, leading to either wasted memory or buffer overflows. The `realloc` function provides a powerful solution, allowing programs to adjust the size of dynamically allocated memory blocks during runtime. This enables efficient handling of data collections that grow or shrink as the program executes.

Your task is to write a C program that reads a sequence of non-negative integers from standard input. The input will terminate when a negative integer is encountered. Store all the non-negative integers in a dynamically allocated array. Initially, allocate memory for 2 integers. If the array becomes full, use `realloc` to double its capacity. After reading all non-negative integers, print them one per line in the order they were entered. Ensure all allocated memory is freed before the program exits. The maximum number of non-negative integers will not exceed 100.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: 10 20 30 40 -1 → array: {10} → {10,20} → {10,20,30} → {10,20,30,40}. Output: 10\n20\n30\n40

tasks.title: Calculate Average of Even Numbers in Dynamic Array
tasks.description:
Dynamic memory allocation is crucial for processing datasets whose size isn't known until runtime. This ensures programs adapt to various input scales without hardcoding array limits, making it ideal for tasks like statistical analysis. By allocating memory as needed, we can efficiently store and analyze data regardless of its volume.

Your task is to write a C program that calculates the average of all even numbers in a dynamically allocated array. First, read an integer `N` (1 <= N <= 100) representing the number of integers. Then, dynamically allocate an array of `N` integers. Read `N` integers into this array. Iterate through the array, identify all even numbers, and calculate their average. If no even numbers are found, print "No even numbers." (followed by a newline). Otherwise, print the average as an integer (truncated, not rounded). Ensure all dynamically allocated memory is freed before the program exits.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: 5\n1 2 3 4 5 → evens: 2 → 2,4. sum: 2 → 6. count: 1 → 2. average: 6/2=3. Output: 3

tasks.title: Concatenate Two Strings into New Dynamic Memory
tasks.description:
String manipulation in C often requires careful memory management, as strings are essentially character arrays. Dynamic memory allocation is vital for creating new strings whose lengths are determined at runtime. This is particularly useful for operations like string concatenation, where the combined length of strings might not be known until the program is running, allowing for flexible and efficient memory use.

Your task is to write a C program that reads two separate strings, `s1` and `s2`, from standard input. Each string will be a single word, with a maximum length of 50 characters. Dynamically allocate memory for a new string that will hold the concatenation of `s1` followed by `s2`. Copy `s1` into the new memory, then append `s2`. Finally, print the resulting concatenated string to standard output, followed by a newline. All dynamically allocated memory must be freed before the program exits.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: Hello World → combined: H → He → Hel... → Hello → HelloW → HelloWo... → HelloWorld. Output: HelloWorld

tasks.title: Manage Student Records with Dynamic Allocation
tasks.description:
Structures are powerful tools for grouping related data. When you need to manage a variable number of such records, for instance, a list of students in a class, dynamically allocating an array of structures becomes indispensable. This approach allows for efficient memory usage, adapting to the exact number of records needed, similar to how database systems handle flexible data storage.

Your task is to write a C program to manage student records. Define a `struct Student` with two members: `int id` and `char* name`. Read an integer `N` (1 <= N <= 5), representing the number of student records. Dynamically allocate an array of `N` `struct Student` objects. For each student, read their `id` (integer) and `name` (a single word string, max 20 chars). For each student's `name` member, dynamically allocate memory to store the name. After reading all records, print each student's `id` and `name` on a new line, separated by a space. All dynamically allocated memory must be freed before the program exits.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input N=2; Student1: 101 Alice; Student2: 102 Bob → records: [{101, "Alice"}] → [{101, "Alice"}, {102, "Bob"}]. Output: 101 Alice\n102 Bob

test_results.actual:
Enter ID for student 1: Enter name for student 1: Enter ID for student 2: Enter name for student 2: 101 Alice
102 Bob
Enter ID for student 1: Enter name for student 1: 205 Charlie

Enter ID for student 1: Enter name for student 1: Enter ID for student 2: Enter name for student 2: Enter ID for student 3: Enter name for student 3: 301 David
302 Eve
303 Frank

tasks.title: Find Maximum Value and Index in a Dynamic Array
tasks.description:
Dynamically allocated arrays are frequently used to store data whose size is determined during program execution. Efficiently searching such arrays, for example, to find the maximum element and its location, is a fundamental operation in data processing. This capability allows programs to handle varying amounts of data without imposing rigid, compile-time size constraints.

Your task is to write a C program that finds the maximum value and the index of its *first* occurrence in a dynamically allocated array. First, read an integer `N` (1 <= N <= 100). Then, dynamically allocate an array of `N` integers. Read `N` integers into this array. Find the maximum value in the array and the index of its first occurrence (0-based). Print the maximum value, followed by a space, then its index. Ensure all dynamically allocated memory is freed before the program exits.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: 5\n10 50 20 50 30 → max: 10 → 50. index: 0 → 1. Output: 50 1

tasks.title: Count Character Occurrences in a Dynamic String
tasks.description:
Working with strings often involves analyzing their content. Dynamic strings allow flexibility when the exact length of the text being analyzed is not fixed beforehand, which is a common scenario in text processing applications. By allocating memory for the string at runtime, programs can efficiently handle text of varying lengths without arbitrary buffer limits.

Your task is to write a C program that counts the occurrences of a specific character within a dynamically allocated string. First, read a single line of text (a sentence, including spaces, up to 100 characters long). Dynamically allocate memory to store this string. Then, read a single character from the next line. Count how many times this character appears in the dynamically allocated string (case-sensitive). Print the total count to standard output, followed by a newline. All dynamically allocated memory must be freed before the program exits.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: Hello World\nl → count: 0 → 1 → 2 → 3. Output: 3

tasks.title: Reverse Word Order in a Dynamically Allocated Sentence
tasks.description:
Processing sentences often requires breaking them down into individual words. Pointers and dynamic memory are crucial for storing these words, especially when the number of words or their individual lengths are not known until runtime. This approach enables flexible text manipulation, such as reordering words, without being constrained by fixed-size buffers.

Your task is to write a C program that reads a single line of text (a sentence) from standard input, up to 100 characters long. Assume words are separated by single spaces. Dynamically allocate an array of `char*` pointers. For each word in the sentence, dynamically allocate memory to store that word and store its pointer in the array. Print the words in reverse order, each followed by a space, and a newline at the end. If the input is an empty line (only a newline character), print only a newline. All dynamically allocated memory must be freed before the program exits.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: This is a test sentence → words: ["This"] → ["This", "is"] → ... → ["This", "is", "a", "test", "sentence"]. Output: sentence test a is This\n

test_cases.input: a b c d e f
test_cases.expected_output: f e d c b a

test_results.input: a b c d e f
test_results.expected: f e d c b a
test_results.actual: f e d c b a
